title: The Greatest Salesman in the World
author: Og Mandino

chapters.title: Know why you want to sell before you sell anything
chapters.text:
Most people misunderstand what it takes to persuade others effectively. Some chase income, others chase status, yet only a minority study the craft itself. The opening obstacle is ignorance: sellers often lack a method for earning trust.

Mastery asks for wholehearted commitment, steady action, and spiritual seriousness. It requires humility, internal calm, and an honest appraisal of personal value. Ideas travel only when the ethics of exchange are respected.

Og Mandino retells biblical material to show selling as stewardship rather than trickery. Readers seeking the full path from apprentice to legend will find that arc developed here.

Mandino insists that selling is persuasion in service of something larger than commission. Without purpose, technique becomes manipulation; with purpose, technique becomes mentorship.

chapters.title: Hafid's locked room and the scrolls of success
chapters.text:
In antiquity the trader Hafid summoned Erasmus, his most trusted aide, and ordered a full inventory of his holdings. Though celebrated as Earth's finest salesman, he stunned Erasmus by instructing him to convert nearly every asset into gold talents for charity, reserving only enough for a modest retirement. When Erasmus protested that profits had never been higher, Hafid asked for patience while he honored a vow made decades earlier to his teacher.

Erasmus complied. Hafid unlocked a chamber sealed since their partnership began. A humble chest inside contained ten leather scrolls. Each parchment held a principle that had built his empire—wisdom meant for a single heir who would arrive bearing a sign.

The scrolls symbolize knowledge too potent for careless distribution. Hafid's youth confirms the rule.

As a shepherd for Pathros, he once knelt and begged training in commerce. Pathros questioned how wealth would be spent; Hafid pledged generosity mirroring his mentor. Pathros consented, sold him an embroidered red robe for one silver denarius, and dispatched him to Bethlehem to resell it at profit. Before the journey he emphasized endurance with these words:

"Failure will never overtake you if your determination to succeed is strong enough."

Erasmus's confusion mirrors every apprentice who discovers that wealth without wisdom is hollow. Hafid's decision to give away his fortune sets the moral frame for the scrolls: success must fund meaning.

In ancient times, a wealthy man named Hafid called his closest servant, Erasmus, and told him to estimate the worth of everything he owned. Hafid was a wealthy man who was seen as the greatest salesman in the world.

He had riches of all kinds, and he lived in affluence. It came as a rude shock to Erasmus, however, when Hafid told him to convert his vast fortune to gold talents and to donate it to the poor, leaving only enough for Hafid to live for the remaining days he had left to live.

Confused, Erasmus reminded Hafid that the past year had been the most profitable year they’ve had. The wealthy man asked his servant to trust him and to stay with him until he fulfilled a promise he had made to his own master when he was still young.

chapters.title: When giving away the robe was the real sale
chapters.text:
Sharp traders distinguish sacrifice from waste.

Hafid traveled by donkey yet found no buyer in Bethlehem. One frigid night he discovered a couple sheltering a newborn in a cave, sharing thin cloaks for warmth. Compassion moved him to clothe the infant in the ceremonial robe so the parents could cover themselves.

Their gratitude was profound. Returning homeward, Hafid marveled at a radiant star tracking his path across the heavens.

Pathros inquired about the celestial light and the mission's outcome. Convinced he had disappointed his teacher, Hafid confessed failure to monetize the garment. Pathros celebrated instead of rebuking him.

Conduct beyond the ledger often persuades observers more than any rehearsed pitch.

Character colors your definition of victory. Selflessness signals maturity.

Soon afterward Pathros lay dying. He described rescuing a wise traveler from bandits and receiving a cedar chest of scrolls. Obedience to their teachings, he promised, would crown Hafid a master seller. Pathros bequeathed the texts plus one hundred gold talents for rug trading in Damascus, assuring him that disciplined practice would yield premium prices.

Did you know? A 2013 study by the International Finance Corporation identified that 90% of jobs in developing countries are in the private sector.

Pathros recognized that the robe's value lay in the compassion it enabled, not the price it might fetch. That lesson precedes every scroll Hafid later memorized: character closes deals hearts remember.

One of the greatest pieces of business advice is to know when to sacrifice something and when not to. Hafid rode to Bethlehem on a donkey to sell the robe, but he was unsuccessful.

One night, however, he found a man and a woman inside a cold cave with nothing to cover their body. The two of them had an infant with them whom they covered with their cloaks.

chapters.title: Scrolls one through five: renewal, love, grit, dignity, time
chapters.text:
The opening half of Hafid's collection ties calendar discipline to unshakable belief in personal worth. Each scroll is a contract with the future self.

"Today I begin a new life."

Yesterday's errors need not dictate tomorrow's behavior. Audit habits, draft a roadmap for revenue and character, and practice virtues until they feel automatic. It is never too late to replan.

Focus on executing the map instead of mourning detours. Sales targets and ethical aims can share the same schedule when intentions are honest.

"I will greet this day with love in my heart."

Love here means operational warmth: greet clients, rivals, and strangers without contempt. Happiness is a professional asset because people buy from moods they trust.

Let compassion guide daylight and reflection guide night. Treat obstacles as tuition, not punishment. Open hearts close fewer deals through hostility.

"I will persist until I succeed."

Rewards arrive after effort, never before it. Patience separates career sellers from tourists. Failure is a stepping stone when analyzed instead of feared.

Advance one call, one rehearsal, one follow-up at a time. Celebrate micro-wins to fuel macro goals.

"I am nature's greatest miracle."

Self-deprecation is not humility; it is sabotage. You are irreplaceable equipment in the universe's toolkit. Carry yourself as someone buyers are fortunate to meet.

Confidence is manufactured through repetition, not conferred by applause. Never hide gifts because critics might stare.

"I will live this day as if it is my last."

Time is inventory that cannot be restocked. Begin the uncomfortable conversation today. Stop renting space in past regrets.

Mandino prescribes reading each scroll twice daily for ten consecutive days before moving on. Liturgy becomes reflex, and reflex becomes reputation.

Avoid discrimination in any form; buyers feel bias instantly. See bad situations as experience that sharpens skill. You are responsible for the confidence you radiate—manufacture it daily through practice, not praise.

Hafid did not treat the scrolls as inspirational posters. He lived them as sequential training: first identity, then affection, then endurance, then dignity, then urgency. That order matters because charisma without discipline collapses under rejection, and discipline without love becomes mechanical.

When Hafid memorized these lines, he was not performing virtue for Pathros; he was building reflexes that would survive cold markets and lonely roads. That is the hidden curriculum of the first five scrolls.

Practice turns language into posture.

Practice turns language into posture.

chapters.title: Scrolls six through ten: emotions, humor, value, action, prayer
chapters.text:
"Today I will be master of my emotions."

Temperament sets the sales floor's climate. Map triggers and script countermoves: sing through melancholy, laugh through sorrow, study rivals when complacency whispers. Feelings inform you; they should not command you.

"I will laugh at the world."

Gravity has its place, yet obsession over unsolvable troubles drains capacity. Determination outlasts most problems. Balance diligence with delight.

"Today I will multiply my value a hundredfold."

Capacity scales when effort compounds. Improve offerings, extend reach, and communicate quality without vanity. Humility keeps expansion humane.

"I will act now."

Strategy without motion is decoration. Postponement invoices interest on dreams. The present moment is the only workshop you own.

"I will pray for guidance."

Seek wisdom beyond ego through prayer or contemplation. Request direction, court serenity, and remember that moving hearts is sacred labor.

Great selling remains attainable for anyone who rehearses these disciplines sincerely.

Emotional discipline keeps rejection from defining identity. Laughing at setbacks prevents them from colonizing imagination. Action and prayer bookend the day so ego does not become the only voice heard.

Make the best use of every situation you find yourself. Being a great salesman is possible as long as you are committed to achieving your goals.

Scroll six trains emotional reflexes before they sabotage meetings. Scroll seven prevents catastrophizing from draining creativity. Scroll eight demands continuous improvement of offer and self. Scroll nine attacks procrastination directly. Scroll ten roots ambition in humility by asking for guidance beyond ego.

Together the final five scrolls argue that inner weather determines outer results. Master mood, mock despair, multiply skill, move immediately, and stay oriented toward something larger than commission.

Read these scrolls with the same cadence Hafid used: slow mornings, honest inventory, immediate action.

Read these scrolls with the same cadence Hafid used: slow mornings, honest inventory, immediate action.

chapters.title: Paul arrives with the robe that completed the circle
chapters.text:
Living the scrolls, Hafid amassed renown and years. Elderly now, he awaited the successor prophecy promised. A travel-worn man asked whether he stood before the planet's greatest salesman. Hafid assented; the visitor named himself Saul of Tarsus, formerly an enforcer hunting disciples of Jesus.

Saul—later Paul—recounted lightning on the Damascus road, blindness, and a voice accusing his cruelty. Guided into the city, he met Ananias, who restored sight invoking Christ. Convinced, Paul abandoned persecution for preaching. The voice then commanded him to locate the supreme salesman and learn to proclaim truth.

During dialogue Paul unveiled a bloodied red robe. Hafid instantly recognized the garment from Bethlehem and trembled with joy.

Hearing Paul's account echo the nativity night, Hafid summoned Erasmus: "Faithful friend, bring the cedar chest. Our heir has come."

Paul's willingness to evangelize despite peril proved he could market conviction the way Hafid marketed goods.

Ceilings lift when integrity and audacity align.

"Never feel shame for trying and failing for he who has never failed is he who has never tried." ~ Og Mandino

The blood-stained robe links Hafid's youthful gift with Paul's mission, proving that generosity compounds across generations. Succession in the story is spiritual as much as commercial.

Hafid followed the principles of the scrolls as suggested by the Wiseman, and he became the greatest salesman in the world. Now elderly, it was time for him to pass on the scroll to another worthy person.

One fateful day, a haggard-looking man approached him and asked him if he was the greatest salesman in the world. Hafid answered in the affirmative and the man introduced himself as Saul of Tarsus.

Saul explained to him that he was a persecutor who had been ordered to look for the followers of Jesus and take them to Jerusalem for punishment. Saul, now called Paul, since an incident, told Hafid that as he was journeying to Damascus, he was struck by lightning, and he was blinded while a voice spoke to him.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Hafid's narrative links generosity with commercial greatness. Moving ideas—whether products or beliefs—depends on principles top performers internalize.

Dedication precedes reputation. Falls are data, not verdicts. Persistence keeps momentum alive.

Mandino anchors the lesson in scripture to stress patience, reverence for hours, resolve, and compassion.

The scrolls speak to character formation and civic virtue, not merely closing deals.

Try this:
Read the content of each scroll at least twice a day for a period of ten days. Put the content into practice and reward yourself consistently when you successfully apply the content to your daily life.

Mandino leaves readers with homework: repeat each scroll until its language shapes reflexes. Mastery is repetition with reflection, not a single inspirational afternoon.

The story of Hafid tells the importance of selfless acts, and how to become a great salesman. Selling ideas or beliefs to people requires several principles that must not be ignored.

The world’s greatest people know these principles, and they follow it very well. They know that the first step to being a successful salesman is to know what it takes to sell.
